channel.title: NPR Topics: Science
channel.description: The latest health and science news. Updates on medicine, healthy living, nutrition, drugs, diet, and advances in science and technology. Subscribe to the Health & Science podcast.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: The Artemis II crew saw parts of the moon never seen before. Here's what they said
item.description: The astronauts on Artemis II observed parts of the moon humans had never seen before. Their findings provide a scientific baseline — and sense of wonder — for future missions.
item.content:encoded: The astronauts on Artemis II observed parts of the moon humans had never seen before. Their findings provide a scientific baseline — and sense of wonder — for future missions.
item.dc:creator: Ava Berger

item.title: What the Artemis II mission might mean for the future of lunar research
item.description: NASA's Artemis II crew is on their way home. What will the mission mean for the future of lunar research? Astronomer and professor Jack Burns weighs in.
item.content:encoded: NASA's Artemis II crew is on their way home. What will the mission mean for the future of lunar research? Astronomer and professor Jack Burns weighs in.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: A 500 million year old fossil could change what we understand about spiders
item.description: Pincers found on a tiny fossil that lived 500 million years ago could change how scientists understand the origin of spiders.
item.content:encoded: Pincers found on a tiny fossil that lived 500 million years ago could change how scientists understand the origin of spiders.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: Astronauts suggest naming a moon crater 'Carroll' after their commander's late wife
item.description: The Artemis II crew, led by Reid Wiseman, was the first to lay eyes on several craters on the far side of the moon. The astronauts want to name one of them after Carroll Wiseman, who died of cancer in 2020.
item.content:encoded:
The Artemis II crew, led by Reid Wiseman, was the first to lay eyes on several craters on the far side of the moon. The astronauts want to name one of them after Carroll Wiseman, who died of cancer in 2020.

(Image credit: NASA via Getty Images)
item.dc:creator: Rachel Treisman

item.title: Photos: NASA releases first images from moon flyby
item.description: During the mission's loop around the moon, the crew took geological observations of places of interest on the lunar surface using their own eyes and snapping thousands of photos of the surface.
item.content:encoded: During the mission's loop around the moon, the crew took geological observations of places of interest on the lunar surface using their own eyes and snapping thousands of photos of the surface.
item.dc:creator: NPR Staff

item.title: Artemis II astronauts swung by the moon, broke an Apollo record, and saw an eclipse
item.description: The NASA moon mission completed several key milestones as its crew looped around the lunar body. It's expected to splash down on Earth on Friday.
item.content:encoded: The NASA moon mission completed several key milestones as its crew looped around the lunar body. It's expected to splash down on Earth on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Brendan Byrne

item.title: What can Artemis II astronauts see that satellites haven't captured?
item.description: The astronauts on Artemis II will observe parts of the moon rarely seen by human eyes. A NASA planetary scientist said it will offer a vital perspective for lunar research.
item.content:encoded:
The astronauts on Artemis II will observe parts of the moon rarely seen by human eyes. A NASA planetary scientist said it will offer a vital perspective for lunar research.

(Image credit: NASA)
item.dc:creator: Ava Berger

item.title: NASA's Artemis II astronauts ready for their close-up view of the Moon
item.description: Monday is an important day on NASA's Artemis II mission. The crew is preparing for its lunar flyby and setting a record along the way: the farthest that humans have ventured in space.
item.content:encoded: Monday is an important day on NASA's Artemis II mission. The crew is preparing for its lunar flyby and setting a record along the way: the farthest that humans have ventured in space.
item.dc:creator: Nell Greenfieldboyce

item.title: Artemis II astronauts heading home after historic moon flyby
item.description: The Artemis II astronauts are on a course back to Earth after flying by the moon Monday, witnessing a solar eclipse, and breaking a distance record set by NASA's Apollo 13 astronauts.
item.content:encoded:
The Artemis II astronauts are on a course back to Earth after flying by the moon Monday, witnessing a solar eclipse, and breaking a distance record set by NASA's Apollo 13 astronauts.

(Image credit: NASA via Getty Images)
item.dc:creator: Brendan Byrne

item.title: These rock-climbing fish can shimmy up a 50-foot waterfall
item.description: New research from the Democratic Republic of Congo offers a behavioral and anatomical portrait of a species that can achieve surprising athletic feats.
item.content:encoded:
New research from the Democratic Republic of Congo offers a behavioral and anatomical portrait of a species that can achieve surprising athletic feats.

(Image credit: Pacifique Kiwele Mutambala)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel
